Q1: I tried to keep a pretty regular study routine, honestly. I'd block out time most days, usually in the afternoons, and I'd sit down with my materials for a few hours. The problem is that I'd go through the motions but I wasn't really absorbing things. I'm someone who needs to do things with my hands, practice problems, build stuff, but a lot of the course was just reading and watching, which made it hard to stay focused even when I was technically studying.
Q2: I tried to keep regular times, like studying after lunch most days. But when deadlines came up, everything got chaotic and I'd cram more. The routine was there on paper, but the quality of what I was doing varied a lot. Some weeks I'd put in my 28 hours but feel like I got nothing out of it because I was just scared and going through motions.
Q3: Usually it was because I knew I was supposed to. Like, I'd tell myself I need to check if there's something due or if the professor posted anything new. Sometimes it was fear, honestly, that scared feeling that I might be missing something important. I'd log in, scroll around, maybe watch part of a video, then log out feeling like I hadn't really accomplished anything meaningful.
Q4: Yeah, for a while logging in became automatic, like something I just did in the morning with my coffee. It happened because I was trying to stay on top of things and not fall behind. But even though it was routine, I wasn't really engaging deeply. It was more like checking a box than actually learning anything substantial.
Q5: The videos were okay because I could at least see and hear someone explaining things, which is better than just reading. I also liked when there were practice problems or anything hands-on. Discussion posts were sometimes useful because I could see how other people were thinking about the material. But honestly, nothing felt perfectly suited to how I learn best.
Q6: I ignored a lot of the reading materials and the longer text-based resources. I'd open them, skim a bit, and then close them because I just couldn't focus on walls of text. The optional supplementary stuff I barely touched because I was already struggling with the required materials. It felt overwhelming and I didn't have the energy to go beyond what was mandatory.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could actually do something with it, like work through a problem or apply a concept. If it was just passive consumption, I'd zone out pretty quickly. I needed to feel like I was making progress or building toward something, not just absorbing information that would slip away the moment I closed the tab.
Q8: Optional activities felt like extras I couldn't afford to spend time on when I was already drowning with the required stuff. Required activities had this weight to them, like I had to do them or face consequences. But that pressure didn't always translate to better engagement, it just made me more stressed and scared about falling behind.
Q9: Yeah, I downloaded a lot of materials and tried to study from printed notes or my own handwritten stuff. I learn better when I can physically write things out or work with materials in my hands. So a lot of my studying happened with notebooks and printed PDFs spread out on my desk, which the LMS would never capture.
Q10: There were plenty of times I'd be at my desk for hours working through problems by hand or rewriting notes to understand concepts better. The LMS would just show that I logged in once or watched a video, but the real work was happening offline. I'd also talk through problems out loud to myself, which helped but obviously doesn't show up anywhere.
Q11: Definitely. I'd download a PDF or save a video to watch later, and then I'd go back to those files multiple times without ever logging in again. It was just easier to have everything offline where I could access it without dealing with the platform. The LMS probably thought I only looked at something once when really I studied it repeatedly.
Q12: There were definitely moments when everything felt impossible. When I got my first few assignment scores back and they weren't great, I got really scared and my motivation tanked even though I was still stressed about it. The fear made it hard to focus, and I'd sit there trying to study but my mind would just spiral about failing.
Q13: Absolutely. When I was exhausted or had other things going on, the LMS became this thing I'd just click through without really absorbing anything. Confusion was a big one too, if I didn't understand something, I'd get frustrated and disengage rather than pushing through. The fatigue made everything feel like it took twice as much effort.
Q14: All the time, honestly. I'd have a video playing but my mind would be somewhere else entirely, worrying about my grades or just feeling overwhelmed. I'd scroll through discussion posts without really reading them. My eyes were on the screen but my brain had checked out. It was like being there physically but not mentally.
Q15: I postponed assignments constantly, even when I knew they mattered. The fear of doing them wrong made me avoid starting them. I'd tell myself I'd do it tomorrow when I felt more prepared, but that day never really came. By the time I finally attempted things, I was rushing and not doing my best work, which just made everything worse.
Q16: Deadlines were the only thing that really forced me to engage with the LMS. I'd suddenly become very active in the days before something was due, cramming and trying to catch up. But that panic-driven engagement wasn't quality learning. It was survival mode, just trying to submit something rather than actually understanding the material.
Q17: Feedback honestly scared me more than it helped. When I got negative feedback, I'd feel terrible and want to avoid the course entirely for a while. It didn't motivate me to work harder, it just made me more anxious about trying again. I know that's not the right response, but that fear was hard to overcome.
Q18: The structure felt pretty rigid and didn't really accommodate different learning styles. Everything was laid out in a way that assumed you'd just read and watch and then somehow know the material. For someone like me who needs to physically engage with content, the structure made it harder to find my footing and stay motivated.
Q19: The lack of hands-on activities made engagement really hard for me. If there had been more interactive elements or practical applications, I think I would have done better. The discussion posts were okay but they felt forced sometimes. The course design assumed everyone learns the same way, which just isn't true.
Q20: I think that describes me pretty well actually. I was logging in, watching videos, participating in discussions, but my assignment completion was terrible. The activity was there because I was trying, but the fear and confusion made it hard to actually produce quality work. Sometimes being active doesn't mean being effective.
Q21: That's not really my experience since I had both low activity and poor performance. But I can see how someone who learns efficiently offline might not need the LMS much. If they're reading textbooks or studying in groups in person, their real engagement wouldn't show up in the data at all.
Q22: Course logs can show when you're present and what you click on, but they can't show if you're actually understanding anything. They miss all the offline studying, the mental struggles, the fear that keeps you from performing well. They see the surface but not what's happening underneath, which is where the real story is.
Q23: Being engaged means actually connecting with the material, not just showing up. It means understanding concepts and being able to apply them, feeling like you're making progress. For me, real engagement would involve doing something active with the content, not just passively consuming it. It's about your mind being present, not just your login.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how scared I was most of the time, or how that fear paralyzed me even when I wanted to succeed. It wouldn't show the hours I spent studying offline with handwritten notes, or the mindfulness exercises I tried to calm myself down. It captures clicks and timestamps but misses the whole emotional experience of struggling through a course.
